nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoons, inner reefs, and rocky crevices in full-strength Caribbean seawater; typically buries its pedal disc in sand or wedges into rock crevices

decorPreferences: sand substrate for pedal disc anchoring

diet.notes: Target-feed meaty marine foods 1–2 times per week. Suitable foods include silversides, krill, mussels, chopped fish, and raw shrimp. Place food directly on the tentacles using a feeding stick or pipette. The anemone also supplements its nutrition through zooxanthellae photosynthesis. Inadequate feeding has been associated with tentacles appearing stringy or deflated.

goodWith: non-aggressive fish too large to be stung
compatible hermit crabs (avoid Red-Legged Hermit Crabs which may predate it)
avoidWith: clownfish (does not host; may eat them)
small fish that can fit in its mouth

summary: The Haitian Reef Anemone (Condylactis gigantea) is a large, vibrant Caribbean anemone with colorful tentacle tips. Despite a common hobby misconception, it does not host clownfish and will readily eat small fish and ornamental shrimp.
careNotes:
Condylactis gigantea is a photosynthetic carnivore native to the Caribbean Sea and western Atlantic, inhabiting shallow lagoons and inner reefs. It is one of the most commonly available anemones in the saltwater hobby, valued for its striking appearance — tentacles are typically cream, tan, white, or greenish with purple, rose, or pink tips.

IMPORTANT COMPATIBILITY NOTE: Despite a persistent hobby misconception, Condylactis gigantea does NOT naturally host clownfish. It is a Caribbean species, and clownfish/anemone symbiosis evolved exclusively in Indo-Pacific anemone species. While anecdotal reports exist of larger, more aggressive clownfish species (e.g., Maroon or Tomato clownfish) occasionally associating with C. gigantea in captivity, this is not reliable behavior and the anemone is just as likely to capture and consume the clownfish. Do not add clownfish to a tank with this species expecting a hosting relationship.

This species has a potent sting capable of capturing small fish, ornamental shrimp, and harming neighboring corals and other anemones. It will wander unpredictably around the tank and must be given ample space away from other sessile invertebrates. It tends to anchor its pedal disc in sand or a rock crevice, but will relocate if it is unhappy with lighting or flow conditions.

Key care points:
• Lighting: Moderate to strong illumination is generally advised (approximately 75–200 PAR). It is less demanding than bubble-tip or other Indo-Pacific hosting anemones, but still requires a proper reef-grade lighting system. Placing it in the lower-to-middle portions of the tank and allowing it to find its preferred position is recommended.
• Water flow: Moderate, varied flow is preferred — enough for the anemone to sway gently and capture food particles, but not so turbulent that it cannot expand fully.
• Placement: Will anchor wherever it chooses; provide a sand substrate of at least 5 cm depth to protect the pedal disc. The anemone should be given a full section of the tank as it will sting anything within reach.
• Water chemistry: Stable parameters are critical. Requires calcium, magnesium, strontium, iodine, and trace element supplementation as with any photosynthetic invertebrate. Regular partial water changes are strongly advised.
• Tank maturity: Generally advised to add this species to a well-established system (at least 3–6 months old) with stable water chemistry.
• Reproduction: Has been documented reproducing via pedal laceration (asexual) in aquariums. Sexual reproduction involves broadcast spawning in the wild; captive sexual breeding is not commonly practiced.

Sources disagreed modestly on temperature range: LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain cited 22–25.6°C (72–78°F); Reeflings cited 24–28°C (75–82°F). An averaged range of 23–27°C is used here as the practical recommended band.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs by broadcast spawning, with a primary mating season documented around late May. The species is generally dioecious. In captivity, asexual reproduction via pedal laceration (where small pieces of the pedal disc detach and grow into new anemones) has been observed. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order Actiniaria, Family Actiniidae), native range confirmation, physical description (disc up to 40 cm in nature), diet in the wild (macrophagous carnivore: fish, mussels, shrimp, zooplankton, sea worms), and ecology notes. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Reeflings – Condylactis gigantea Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 24–28°C vs. LiveAquaria 22–26°C — averaged to 23–27°C), salinity (1.023–1.026), tank size (100L), adult size (50 cm wild), care level (moderate/intermediate), and clownfish non-hosting behavior. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament and carnivorous diet.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Condy Anemone Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F / 22–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), tank size (30 gal / 114L), dKH (8–12), feeding schedule (every 2–3 days with chopped fish, mussels, shrimp), clownfish non-hosting confirmation, and moderate care level.
